compiled_state.title: Agent bypasses fix-bug skill investigation workflow for behavioral bugs, applies fixes directly
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## What happened

When processing 4 CI failures in sequence via /dso:fix-bug, the agent correctly classified bug 1 (c633-84d2, CLAUDE.md missing strings) as mechanical and followed the Mechanical Fix Path. However, for bug 2 (f57c-6fe3, ticket-concurrency-stress), the agent:

1. Correctly classified it as behavioral (score 2, BASIC tier)
2. Skipped Step 2 (Investigation Sub-Agent Dispatch) entirely
3. Skipped Step 3 (Hypothesis Testing) entirely
4. Skipped Step 3.5 (Hypothesis Validation Gate) entirely
5. Jumped directly to reading the test code and applying a fix
6. Did not write a discovery file

For bugs 3 and 4, the agent began direct investigation (reading CI logs, grepping source code, running commands) rather than dispatching investigation sub-agents.

## Why the agent did this

The agent likely rationalized the bypass because:
- The root cause appeared "obvious" from reading the test code and CI output (compact-on-close reducing event counts below hardcoded thresholds)
- Dispatching a sub-agent felt like overhead for what seemed like a simple threshold adjustment
- The HARD-GATE says "Do NOT modify any code...until Steps 1-5 are complete" but the agent treated its own manual investigation as equivalent to a sub-agent investigation
- Processing 4 bugs sequentially created pressure to move quickly, leading to shortcut-taking

## Why this is wrong

The fix-bug skill's investigation sub-agent dispatch exists for structural reasons:
- Sub-agents follow a rigorous investigation template (five whys, hypothesis generation/elimination, empirical validation)
- The hypothesis validation gate (Step 3.5) prevents speculative fixes — without it, the agent has no confirmed evidence before implementing
- The discovery file protocol creates an audit trail
- The separation between investigation and implementation prevents confirmation bias (the investigator doesn't know it will also be the fixer)

The agent's "obvious" read of the concurrency-stress bug was correct in this instance, but the workflow exists precisely to catch cases where the "obvious" read is wrong.

## Affected tickets
- f57c-6fe3: behavioral bug, investigation skipped, fix applied directly
- 3cb1-00a8: investigation started manually before user interrupted
- c40b-42e6: not yet started but would likely have followed same pattern

## Suggested fix
Strengthen the HARD-GATE enforcement or add a pre-Step-2 checkpoint that explicitly asks: "Am I about to dispatch an investigation sub-agent, or am I about to investigate myself?" The current HARD-GATE blocks code modification but does not block the agent from performing investigation outside of a sub-agent.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Classification: behavioral, Score: 2 (BASIC). Agent bypasses investigation sub-agent dispatch by performing investigation inline — SKILL.md HARD-GATE blocks code modification but not inline investigation.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
